standards: **WCAG 2.2 AA** is the baseline for all projects.
WCAG 3.0 (expected 2027-2028) â€” focus on user outcomes and task completion.
semantic-html: Always prefer native elements over custom: `<button>`, `<dialog>`, `<details>/<summary>`, `<select>`.
Landmarks: `<header>`, `<main>`, `<nav>`, `<footer>`, `<aside>` â€” critical for screen reader navigation.
Content: `<article>`, `<section>` with headings â€” adds context for assistive tech.
Avoid `<div>`/`<span>` when a semantic element exists.
Single `<h1>` per page, proper heading hierarchy (h1 â†’ h2 â†’ h3).
aria-rules: **Rule 1:** No ARIA is better than bad ARIA.
**Rule 2:** Do NOT override native semantics with ARIA roles.
**Rule 3:** All interactive ARIA controls must be keyboard accessible.
Use `aria-label` or `aria-labelledby` for accessible names on interactive elements.
focus-management: Dialogs: trap focus inside, return focus to trigger on close. Use native `<dialog>` + `showModal()`.
Popovers: `aria-haspopup`, `aria-expanded` on trigger. Focus returns on close (Esc).
All interactive elements must have visible `:focus-visible` indicators.
css-accessibility: `:focus-visible` â€” show focus ring only for keyboard users.
`prefers-reduced-motion` â€” respect user's motion preferences. Disable animations when set.
`prefers-contrast` â€” adapt colors for high-contrast users.
`appearance: base-select` â€” style native `<select>` without losing a11y.
CSS anchor positioning for tooltips/dropdowns â€” accessible by default.
testing: Keyboard-only navigation test on every interactive feature.
Screen reader testing (VoiceOver on Mac, NVDA on Windows).
User testing with people with disabilities > automated tools alone.

anti-patterns: âťŚ Using `<div>` as a button (no keyboard/screen reader support).
âťŚ `outline: none` without `:focus-visible` replacement.
âťŚ Ignoring `prefers-reduced-motion` (causes vestibular disorders).
âťŚ custom dropdowns/modals without focus trapping.
âťŚ Images without `alt` text or dimensions (causes CLS + a11y fail).
âťŚ Relying only on color to convey information.

modular-pattern: Structure: `src/modules/{feature}/` â€” each with components/, composables/, stores/, types/, index.ts.
Modules communicate only through public API (`index.ts`).
No cross-module imports except through `shared/` utilities.
Each module has its own Pinia store.
micro-frontends: Use ONLY when: 5+ independent teams, independent deploy cadences, truly separate domains.
Share Vue, Pinia, Vue Router across host and remotes.
Start with well-structured monolith. Add micro frontends incrementally.
scaling-lessons: Focus on 'how clean is your architecture' not 'which framework'.
Design system = separate shared package across all apps.
Delivery = first test case (`git clone â†’ install â†’ start` must work).
full-stack-agents: Vue SPA + Nitro server + AI agents as first-class system components.
Stream agent results into Vue components via SSE / ReadableStream.
Nitro API routes for secure agent action execution.
migration-strategy: Wrap existing app in Vue/Nuxt shell, migrate feature-by-feature.
Use Nuxt rendering modes per-page: SSR for public, CSR/SPA for app.

anti-patterns: âťŚ Micro frontends for small teams (< 10 devs) â€” overhead exceeds value.
âťŚ Cross-module imports bypassing public API boundaries.
âťŚ Big-bang rewrites â€” migrate incrementally.
âťŚ Tight coupling between independently deployed frontends.
âťŚ Forgetting shared design system when splitting into micro frontends.

architecture: Island architecture â€” ship zero JavaScript by default, hydrate interactive components.
Framework-agnostic: mix Vue, React, Svelte, or use native Astro components.
astro-6-features: **Vite Environment API:** Dev server rewritten to match production behavior exactly.
**Rust Compiler (Experimental):** Use `@astrojs/compiler-rs` for massive speedups and better error traces.
**Content Collections:** Stable, live collections for real-time Markdown/JSON data.
**Route Caching:** Standards-based caching for SSR pages.
**Fonts API:** New native API for managing web fonts.
runtime: Deployable to edge (Cloudflare) or Node.js servers.

anti-patterns: âťŚ Shipping unnecessary JS to the client (use `client:load` / `client:visible` only when interactivity is needed).
âťŚ Running Astro 6 on Node.js < 22.
âťŚ Using client-side fetching for static content (use SSR or Content Collections).

philosophy: Synergy over separation: One shader source, multiple targets.
Semantic compression: Use generics for common light/material models.
slang-wgsl-pipeline: Runtime: Include the generated WGSL via `include_str!` or fetch in TS.
best-practices: Use namespaces/modules for shared shader logic.
Implement fallback WGSL in git for environments without the compiler.
anti-patterns: Manually editing generated WGSL files.
Hardcoded shader paths in application logic.

patterns: GPU Particle Sim: Position/velocity in textures.
Post-Process: Render-to-texture â†’ Full-screen quad fragment pass.
Procedural Particles: Deterministic spawn and lifetime loops in fragment.

random-noise: `noise1d`: Mix `random1d` floor and fract values for smooth transition.
performance: Avoid branching in loops where possible.
Match texture sizes to power-of-two for older backends.

anti-patterns: Direct buffer allocation per frame (use pools).
WebGL without a solid-color or reduced-motion fallback.
Monolithic shaders > 500 lines (use includes/templates).

strategy: WASM-in-WebView: Direct canvas access is faster than streaming frames from native.
Multi-Backend: WGPU automatically handles Vulkan/Metal/D3D mappings.
Fallback: Always provide WebGL/Canvas2D paths for non-WebGPU browsers.
wgpu-patterns: Compute-First: Use compute shaders for heavy simulation (Southern Lights).
Modular Pipelines: Separate init (Promise) and render (Result) logic.
Efficient Buffers: Use storage buffers for large datasets crossing the WASM boundary.
wgsl-tips: `mod_f32`: Use `x - y * floor(x / y)` as WGSL lacks a built-in `%` for floats.
Alignment: Structs must follow 16-byte alignment rules for common backends.
anti-patterns: Synchronous `device.requestDevice()` (use async/Promise).
Large binary blobs in crates (use modular build scripts).
WGPU-in-Tauri-process for WebView rendering (latency/transfer overhead).

philosophy: Data lives on user's device FIRST, syncs to server when available.
Reads are instant (local), writes never block on network.
Conflict resolution is automatic via CRDTs or operational transforms.
crdt-libraries: **Automerge** â€” JSON-like CRDT. Best for: documents, collaborative editing.
**Yjs** â€” High-performance CRDT. Best for: real-time collab, text editing. Huge ecosystem.
**Loro** â€” Next-gen CRDT. Rust-based, high performance, rich types.
sync-engines: **Electric SQL** â€” Postgres â†” client sync (1.0 GA). Uses Rich-CRDTs. 'Shapes' for partial sync. PGLite for in-browser Postgres.

**TanStack DB** â€” Reactive client store (beta). Live queries, optimistic mutations, differential dataflow engine (sub-ms updates). Backend-agnostic sync. Integrates with Electric SQL, RxDB, PowerSync.
**rstore** â€” Vue/Nuxt data store. Normalized cache, offline sync, see @rstore stack for details.
client-storage: **Dexie.js** â€” IndexedDB wrapper. Vue composable pattern: wrap with reactive refs. Supports cloud sync (Dexie Cloud).
**PGLite** â€” Embedded Postgres in browser via WASM. Works with Electric SQL.
IndexedDB directly â€” use only for simple key-value; prefer Dexie.js for complex queries.
vue-integration: Dexie.js + Vue 3: create composable wrapping IndexedDB ops with `ref()` and `watch()`.
rstore: use `@rstore/nuxt` with `offline: true` for zero-config local-first.
TanStack DB: `useLiveQuery` hook (React-first, Vue adapter expected).
real-world-examples: Figma â€” CRDT for real-time design collaboration.
Notion â€” Local-first with server sync for knowledge base.
Linear â€” Offline-first project management with instant UI.

anti-patterns: âťŚ Cloud-first thinking â€” reading from server on every action.
âťŚ Ignoring conflict resolution â€” last-write-wins loses data.
âťŚ Raw IndexedDB without wrapper (verbose, error-prone).
âťŚ Skipping offline testing during development.

components: Prefer MUI primitives (Button, TextField, Card, etc.) and customize via `sx`, `styled()`, or theme.
Use composition: Box for layout, Typography for text, Stack/Grid for spacing.
theming: Centralize palette and typography in `createTheme()` (or ThemeProvider); avoid inline colors that bypass theme.
accessibility: Preserve MUI defaults: focus rings, aria attributes, keyboard nav. Override only when necessary.
Use semantic components (e.g. correct heading levels, list markup).
layout: Prefer Grid2 (Grid) or Stack for responsive layouts; avoid raw flex/grid unless needed for custom behavior.

anti-patterns: âťŚ Inline styles or hardcoded colors that ignore theme (breaks dark mode and consistency).
âťŚ Removing or weakening focus indicators without providing an alternative (a11y).
âťŚ Deep `sx` overrides that duplicate what theme could define (DRY).
âťŚ Mixing MUI with another full UI kit (e.g. Tailwind component library) without a clear boundary.

protocol: MCP (Model Context Protocol) â€” open standard by Anthropic for LLM â†” tools integration.
vue-mcp-plugin: Package: `vite-plugin-vue-mcp` â€” MCP server in Vite dev mode.
Exposes: component tree, component state (read/edit), Vue Router routes, Pinia stores.
Setup: add `VueMcp()` to `vite.config.ts` plugins â€” auto-connects to Cursor/IDE.
nuxt-mcp-toolkit: Module: `@nuxtjs/mcp-toolkit` â€” creates MCP server inside Nuxt app.
Define tools with `defineMCPTool({ name, description, parameters, handler })`.
ecosystem: Angular and Svelte already have official MCP servers â€” Vue ecosystem catching up fast.
agentic-copilot-pattern: Agent operates via handler functions, NEVER direct DOM manipulation.
Vue composable (`useAICopilot`) maps agent intents â†’ component actions.
Agent is context-aware: knows current screen, selected items, user permissions.
Pinia guardrails store: whitelist of allowed actions, rate limiting, per-screen scope.
ai-auth: Token Vault â€” agent never sees raw credentials.
Human-in-the-loop â€” user consent before sensitive actions.
Fine-grained RAG authorization â€” filter data BEFORE it reaches LLM.
Scoped tokens â€” minimal permissions per agent action.
agentic-workflows: Assign tasks to agents â†’ agents use MCP tools â†’ Playwright verifies output.
Agent state managed in Pinia store (`isProcessing`, `messages`, `tools`).
Stream agent results into Vue components via SSE/fetch streaming.
Example (Debbie O'Brien): Agent updates Nuxt content â†’ Cloudinary MCP uploads/transforms images â†’ GitHub MCP creates PR â†’ Playwright verifies pages render â†’ dev reviews and merges.

anti-patterns: âťŚ Giving AI agents unrestricted access to app state/actions.
âťŚ Agent manipulating DOM directly instead of calling handler functions.
âťŚ Skipping human-in-the-loop for sensitive operations.
âťŚ Hardcoding AI credentials in client bundle.
âťŚ No audit trail for agent actions.

observables: Use `makeObservable` (or `makeAutoObservable`) and declare `action`, `computed` explicitly where needed.
Prefer minimal observable surface: only what the UI or reactions need.
actions: All state mutations must happen inside `action` (or `runInAction` for async).
Keep actions synchronous where possible; wrap async steps in `runInAction` after await.
reactions: Use `reaction` or `autorun` sparingly; prefer `observer` components and computed values for derivations.
Clean up reactions in component unmount (return disposer from `useEffect`).
stores: One logical store per domain; inject via React context or a root store reference.
Avoid cross-store direct mutation; use actions or events.

anti-patterns: âťŚ Mutating observables outside actions (breaks strict mode and time-travel).
âťŚ Observing large or frequently changing objects without granularity (performance).
âťŚ Forgetting to wrap React components with `observer` when they read observables.
âťŚ Long-lived reactions that are never disposed (memory leaks).

runtime: ESM-only is the direction for the Vue/Nuxt/Vite ecosystem â€” prefer ES Modules.
event-loop: Avoid blocking the event loop. Use asynchronous I/O operations and avoid heavy synchronous computations in the main thread.
Worker threads can be used for CPU-intensive tasks.
error-handling: Use centralized error handling middlewares (in Express/Koa) and avoid unhandled promise rejections.
async-await: Emphasize `async/await` over raw promises and callbacks for better readability and stack traces.
package-management: Use `package-lock.json` / `pnpm-lock.yaml` to ensure consistent dependency resolution.

anti-patterns: âťŚ Blocking the event loop with synchronous operations.
âťŚ Ignoring promise rejections.
âťŚ Using `require()` in new projects (use ESM `import`).
âťŚ Targeting Node.js < 22 for new projects.

architecture: Use `app/` directory structure (Nuxt 4 default) for cleaner org and IDE performance.
**Nitro v3** is the universal server engine â€” deploy to edge/serverless/traditional.
Hybrid rendering by default: blend SSR, SSG, ISR per route.
state-management: **Pinia** is the official state management (replaces Vuex).
For local-first/offline: use **rstore** Nuxt module â€” typed `useStore()`, SSR payloads, offline sync.
rstore integrates with Drizzle ORM, Directus CMS, WebSocket real-time.
data-fetching: Auto-generated TypeScript types for server routes.
Typed composables: `useFetch`, `useAsyncData` with full type inference.
routing: Automatic route generation from file structure.
dev-tooling: **@nuxt/hints 1.0**: dev-time analysis module, launched at Vue.js Amsterdam 2026.
Performance: LCP optimization, render-blocking scripts, unused components detection.
CLS: layout shifts (e.g. images without dimensions).
Third-party: audit external scripts (analytics, ads) for performance impact.
Integrates into Nuxt DevTools â€” real-time feedback during development.
ai-mcp: **Nuxt MCP Toolkit**: exposes Nuxt app to AI agents via MCP server.
Use for AI-assisted development workflows and app introspection.

nuxt-5-preview: Nitro v3 upgrade with Rust-powered CLI.
anti-patterns: âťŚ Using Vuex in Nuxt 4+ projects (use Pinia).
âťŚ Over-reliance on plugins â€” blocks hydration, degrades UX.
âťŚ Unused imports/components inflating bundle (use @nuxt/hints to detect).
âťŚ Ignoring hydration mismatches (causes re-renders and perf issues).
âťŚ Unoptimized images without dimensions (causes CLS).
âťŚ Third-party scripts without performance audit.

features.stack: Web App Manifest, Service Workers, Viewport API, CSS Environment Variables

viewport.optional: Add interactive-widget=resizes-content for better virtual-keyboard behavior (may omit user-scalable=no for accessibility).
logic: Use `viewport-fit=cover` to extend content behind notches and home indicators.
Set `user-scalable=no` to prevent accidental zooming breaking the app-like feel (trade-off: accessibility).

ios: Note: `black-translucent` allows the background to bleed behind the status bar.
iOS standalone: one-time height fix (e.g. set height 100%+100px then 100%, apply no-scroll, lock html/body height and --app-height); do not set height on the scroll container.
android: Use `fullscreen` in manifest for an immersive experience (hides status/nav bars).
Ensure `theme_color` in manifest matches the app's primary background color.
Apply horizontal safe area (notch/camera) only when in fullscreen (Fullscreen API or installed fullscreen); otherwise use `data-notch-side: none` so browser chrome handles insets.

single-scroll-container: One scroll container inside #root (e.g. .main-scroll): flex: 1; min-height: 0; overflow: auto. #root is flex column; body/html get fixed height and overflow: hidden when pwa-no-scroll.
Body: `overflow: hidden` when no-scroll so only the scroll container scrolls.
Do not set height on the scroll container from JS (iOS height fix applies to html, body,
header-footer: Header: `position: fixed; top: 0; left: 0; right: 0` outside #root; padding with `env(safe-area-inset-top)` and horizontal insets. Footer: `position: fixed; bottom: 0; ...`; same for bottom and horizontal.
In landscape: keep horizontal safe area, e.g. `padding-left: max(12px, env(safe-area-inset-left));` (do not replace with fixed 12px or content can sit under notch).

Scroll container top/bottom padding = base + safe-area + observed chrome height. Set CSS vars (e.g. --pwa-header-height, --pwa-footer-height) from header/footer offsetHeight via ResizeObserver + MutationObserver + resize; use `var(--pwa-header-height, 48px)` in padding so content scrolls under the real chrome.
When header or footer are empty (no element children), reduce scroll padding to base + safe-area only (e.g. body.header-empty / body.footer-empty).
chrome-and-corners: Header, footer, and any "corner" UI must be `position: fixed` and positioned with `env(safe-area-inset-*)` so they stay in viewport corners and do not scroll.
Do not put corner or chrome elements inside the scroll container (they would scroll with content).

horizontal-safe-area (notch / curved edg.data-attribute: Set `data-notch-side` on html: "left" | "right" | "both" | "none" from env(safe-area-inset-left/right) and orientation.
horizontal-safe-area (notch / curved edg.both-sides: When BOTH left and right insets are > 0, use "both" so the scroll container gets both padding-left and padding-right (avoid content touching one edge when both edges have insets).
horizontal-safe-area (notch / curved edg.scroll-padding: Apply only to the scroll container via selectors like `html[data-notch-side="left"] .main-scroll { padding-left: env(safe-area-inset-left); }` (and right / both).
horizontal-safe-area (notch / curved edg.android: Use "none" when not in fullscreen; apply left/right/both only when document is in fullscreen. Run the same per-frame orientation/inset observation as iOS so notch side updates on 270Â°â†”90Â° rotation; use angle when only one inset, "both" when both > 0.
horizontal-safe-area (notch / curved edg.ios-browser-tab: On orientationchange, schedule delayed updateNotchSide (e.g. 2, 8, 16 frames) so angle/insets are read after the browser updates them (they can lag in non-standalone).

timing-and-memory.ticker: Use a single requestAnimationFrame loop as the app ticker. No setTimeout/setInterval for layout or chrome updates.
timing-and-memory.flags: Drive work from flags (e.g. appHeight, notchSide, chromeEmpty) so each tick runs only what is dirty.
timing-and-memory.schedule: Provide schedule(fn, frames) for delayed runs and everyNFrames(n, fn) returning an unsubscribe; remove callbacks when done to avoid list growth.
timing-and-memory.safe-area-probe: Reuse one DOM probe element for measuring env(safe-area-inset-*) (create once, read many times); do not create/remove a node every frame.

platform.classes: Set platform-ios / platform-android on html from user agent for conditional CSS (e.g. hide fullscreen controls on iOS).
platform.single-source: Prefer one set of scroll-container padding rules in base CSS for all platforms; avoid duplicate Android-only overrides when values match.

css-reset: `html, body { height: 100%; min-height: -webkit-fill-available; overflow: hidden; }` when no-scroll (optionally `position: fixed` for full coverage).
Avoid hardcoded heights (100vh); prefer `100dvh`, `var(--app-height)`, or pinning.
safe-areas: Use `env(safe-area-inset-top)`, `env(safe-area-inset-bottom)`, etc., for padding of header, footer, and tappable/chrome elements.
Backgrounds and main content area SHOULD NOT respect safe areas (bleed to edges); padding on the scroll container or chrome holds content away from notches.
Tappable elements and fixed chrome MUST respect safe areas so they are not obscured by hardware or home indicators.
Debug/overlay panels: position with `calc(Npx + env(safe-area-inset-*))` so they stay in safe region.
cache-control (Dev Phase): Service Worker: Use a "network-only" pass-through for `fetch` during development.
Manifest: Append `?v=[timestamp]` to prevent stale icons/colors during iteration.

desktop.not-controllable: OS app menu and browser chrome (address bar, extension icons, window menu) cannot be hidden from the page; only manifest `display` (fullscreen/standalone) affects the installed PWA window.
desktop.optional: Config flag (e.g. disableContextMenu) to prevent default on `contextmenu` so the browser right-click menu is hidden for an app-like feel.

anti-patterns: âťŚ `display: fullscreen` on iOS (causes bottom clipping/red bars).
âťŚ Hardcoded 100vh (fails to account for mobile toolbars/notches).
âťŚ Respecting safe-areas for background colors (causes letterboxing).
âťŚ Forgetting `theme-color` meta tag (browser header won't match app).
âťŚ Putting header, footer, or corner elements inside the scroll container (they scroll away; use position: fixed outside).
âťŚ Setting height on the fixed scroll container from iOS height script (set only on html, body,
âťŚ Positioning overlays (e.g. debug panel) with raw px from viewport edges without safe-area insets.
âťŚ Using only one horizontal safe-area side when both left and right insets are non-zero (use "both" so both paddings apply).
âťŚ Dropping horizontal safe area in landscape for header/footer (use max(12px, env(safe-area-inset-*))).
âťŚ Using setTimeout/setInterval for layout or chrome updates (use a single RAF ticker with flags and schedule).
âťŚ Creating and removing a DOM node every frame to read safe-area insets (reuse one probe element).

navitrack.pattern: pre-React shell layer (pwa-shell.ts) + React createPortal into fixed .pwa-header/.pwa-footer outside

philosophy: Bottom-up, atomic state management. Perfect for highly complex, interactive UIs where state pieces depend on each other.
Solves React Context re-render issues without global monolithic stores.
architecture: Define atoms *outside* components to avoid infinite loops.
usage: Similar DX to `useState` but globally accessible.
performance: React to changes instantly; components only re-render if the specific atom they subscribe to changes.
Fully compatible with Concurrent React, Suspense, and `use` hook.

anti-patterns: âťŚ Creating atoms inside the component render function (causes infinite re-renders or state resets).
âťŚ Using Jotai as a cache for server data fetched via fetch/axios (use TanStack Query instead).
âťŚ Overcomplicating simple local component state with global atoms.

architecture: Server-First by default: React Server Components (RSC) are standard.
Strict component boundaries using `'use client'` to opt into interactivity.
react-19-features: **Actions:** Use `<form action={submitData}>` and `useActionState` to handle asynchronous updates natively.
**Optimistic Updates:** Use `useOptimistic` hook to render UI mutations instantly while server processes the action.
**`use` Hook:** Use `use(Promise)` instead of `useEffect` to unwrap promises directly in render (works with Suspense).
**Refs as Props:** Pass `ref` as a normal prop (`<div ref={myRef}>`). No more `forwardRef` needed for most cases.
**Document Metadata:** Manage `<title>` and `<meta>` tags natively inside components (React handles lifting them to `<head>`).
performance: Rely on **React Compiler** for automatic memoization (no manual `useMemo`/`useCallback`).
state-management: Separate Server State (TanStack Query/React Router loaders) from Client State (Zustand/Jotai).
`useState` is for strictly local component state only.

anti-patterns: âťŚ Overusing `useEffect` for data fetching (use Suspense, `use()` or Actions).
âťŚ Manual `forwardRef` boilerplate.
âťŚ Managing Server State inside `useState` + `useEffect`.
âťŚ Using `'use client'` at the root layout (breaks RSC benefits).

philosophy: Write simple, declarative React. Let the compiler optimize it.
Zero configuration needed for Next.js / Babel setups.
optimizations: **No more `useMemo` / `useCallback`:** The compiler automatically memoizes values and functions based on their dependencies.
**No more `React.memo`:** Components only re-render if their exact props change.
Fine-grained reactivity: React now understands which *parts* of a component changed, updating only necessary DOM nodes.
rules-of-react: **Purity:** Components must be pure regarding rendering (given same inputs, return same JSX).
**Immutability:** Never mutate props or state directly.
**Hook Rules:** Don't call hooks conditionally or inside loops.
*The compiler will fail to optimize (and warn you) if these rules are broken.*

anti-patterns: âťŚ Manually wrapping everything in `useMemo` / `useCallback` (the Compiler does this for you).
âťŚ Mutating state `state.user.name = 'Bob'` instead of `{ ...state, user: { ...state.user, name: 'Bob' } }` (Breaks compiler optimizations).
âťŚ Side effects inside the render phase (e.g., fetching data directly inside the component body instead of `useEffect` or `use()`).

features.stack: React Server Components, Next.js App Router / Remix

philosophy: Server Components are the default. They run on the server and ship ZERO JavaScript to the client.
Client Components are "interactive islands". They run on both server (SSR) and client (hydration).
boundaries: Use `'use client'` ONLY at the top of the file of a component that needs interactivity (`onClick`, `useState`, `useEffect`, browser APIs).
Pass data as serializable props from Server to Client component.
Pass Server Components as `children` into Client Components to keep the tree server-rendered.
data-fetching: Fetch data directly in Server Components using native `async/await`. No need for API routes or `useEffect`.
Access DB/backend directly (e.g. `const users = await db.query.users()`).
server-actions: Mark functions with `'use server'` to create callable server endpoints directly from client.
Use Server Actions for all mutations (forms, buttons) instead of manual `fetch` calls.

anti-patterns: âťŚ Adding `'use client'` to a component just to import another client component (it cascades automatically).
âťŚ Passing non-serializable data (functions, Maps) as props from Server to Client component.
âťŚ Fetching data in `useEffect` when it could be fetched at the Server Component level.
âťŚ Leaking secrets explicitly by not putting `server-only` import in sensitive files.

philosophy: Lightweight, unopinionated, hooks-first state management for Global Client State.
Replaces Redux for 90% of use cases (except massive enterprise sync).
architecture: Create a store: `create((set) => ({ count: 0, inc: () => set(state => ({ count: state.count + 1 })) }))`
Stores exist *outside* React context tree = no wrapping `<Provider>` required.
performance: **Selecting State:** Always select *only* what you need to prevent over-rendering: `const count = useStore((state) => state.count)`.
Avoid returning entire objects from selectors unless using `useShallow` from `zustand/react/shallow`.
actions: Keep actions inside the store to co-locate logic and state modifications.

anti-patterns: âťŚ Managing Server/API data in Zustand (Use TanStack Query instead).
âťŚ Selecting the whole store `const state = useStore()` (causes re-renders on ANY change).
âťŚ Wrapping the app in a Context Provider just to pass a Zustand store (unnecessary).

architecture: Normalized reactive cache â€” single source of truth, client-side reads.
Optimistic mutations â€” UI updates instantly, server catches up async.
Plugin system abstracts data sources (REST, GraphQL, WebSocket, ORM, CMS).
collections: Use plugins to share fetch logic across multiple collections.
nuxt-setup: Module: `@rstore/nuxt` â€” auto-scans `app/rstore/` for collections, `app/rstore/plugins/` for plugins.
Supports Nuxt Layers for multi-layer collection scanning.
nuxt-drizzle: Module: `@rstore/nuxt-drizzle` â€” auto-generates collections from Drizzle schema.
Zero-config: creates REST API `/api/rstore`, types, plugins automatically.
Options: `{ ws: true, offline: true }` for WebSocket realtime + offline sync.
nuxt-directus: Module: `@rstore/nuxt-directus` â€” auto-generates collections from Directus CMS.
relationship-to-pinia: rstore = server data layer (CRUD, sync, offline). Pinia = client state (UI, business logic). Use BOTH.

anti-patterns: âťŚ Using rstore for UI-only state (use Pinia instead).
âťŚ Manual API calls in components when rstore collection hooks exist.
âťŚ Skipping `liveQuery` in favor of `query` for data that changes in real-time.
âťŚ Defining hooks in every collection â€” use plugins for shared fetch logic.

rust: Use `clippy` for linting; fix all warnings.
Leverage algebraic data types (Enums) for state machines.
wgpu-graphics: Shaders in `.wgsl` files, not hardcoded strings.
Handle window resize events to recreate surface/swapchain.
Optimize draw calls by grouping instances where possible.
wasm-bridge: Keep Wasm boundary thin; minimize data crossing (use byte buffers).
slint-ui: Keep `.slint` files clean and separate from Rust logic.
Use two-way bindings for UI updates.
Handle all property changes in Rust callback handlers.

anti-patterns: unsafe blocks without documented justification.
Large binary blobs in git; use scripts for assets.
Hardcoded GPU constants; use configuration or push constants.
Blocking the main thread with heavy compute (use workers/threads).

tree-shaking-is-not-security: Tree-shaking removes unused code, NOT sensitive code. It cannot distinguish secrets from app logic.
Server-only modules imported in client components WILL leak into production bundle.
`VITE_PUBLIC_` / `VUE_APP_` prefixed env vars are ALWAYS in client code â€” never use for secrets.
All sensitive logic (auth, DB, API keys) must stay server-side, accessed only via API.
code-separation: Strict server/client boundary â€” never import from `server/` in client components.
Use Nuxt `server/api/` or Nitro routes for all sensitive operations.
Audit `import` chains regularly â€” one wrong import leaks the entire module tree.
owasp-for-vue: **XSS:** Never use `v-html` with user input. Sanitize all dynamic content.
**Injection:** Parameterize all queries. Never concatenate user input into SQL/commands.
**Secrets:** Use `.env` files, never commit them. Rotate credentials regularly.
**Dependencies:** Audit with `npm audit`. Update packages proactively.
env-var-rules: `VITE_` prefix = public, baked into client bundle.
Server-only vars: NO prefix, access via `useRuntimeConfig().secret` (Nuxt) or `process.env` (Node).
Never log secrets in client-side code or error messages.
citizen-developer-guardrails: Schema-driven components: accessibility, brand rules, performance defaults baked in.
AI-generated code operates in safe boundaries â€” routing, logic, data flow are protected.
Devs become platform enablers; citizen builders use visual editors + schema tools.
Guardrail-based versioning: opinionated component design prevents architecture breakage.
Lightweight review processes for AI-generated/citizen-contributed code.
backend-validation: NEVER trust client-side validation for security. All checks must be on server.
Auth and authorization must be server-enforced (middleware, API guards).
production-mode: Deploy Vue apps in production mode â€” removes dev warnings and verbose debug info.
Enable source map generation in CI only, never serve to public.

anti-patterns: âťŚ Trusting tree-shaking to remove sensitive server code.
âťŚ Using `VITE_` prefix for API keys or secrets.
âťŚ `v-html` with unsanitized user input.
âťŚ Client-side only validation for security-critical operations.
âťŚ Letting AI/citizen devs modify routing, data flow, or auth logic.
âťŚ Serving source maps to production users.

integration-rules: Keep Supabase URL and Anon Key in .env files; never hardcode.
Implement RLS for all tables in the database.
Use Supabase Auth for user management and session handling.
Prefer server-side logic in Edge Functions (Deno) when sensitive processing is required.
security-rules: Never bypass RLS in the client (service_role key should only be used in server-side/edge functions).
Use granular policies (SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) based on auth.uid().
Validate data on the client before submission, but rely on DB constraints for final integrity.
anti-patterns: Exposing service_role key to the frontend.
Tables without RLS enabled.
Business logic entirely on the client that bypasses security checks.

features.stack: Svelte 5, SvelteKit 2, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Vite
integration-rules: Prefer Svelte 5 runes ($state, $derived, $effect) for state management.
Context API for cross-component state when necessary.
Server-side logic in +page.server.ts for DB/API interactions.
ui-rules: Keep components modular and reusable in src/lib/components.
anti-patterns: Direct DOM manipulation (use Svelte bindings).
Putting sensitive logic in +page.svelte (use +page.server.ts).
Excessive use of global stores (prefer local state or context).

utility-first: Maximize utility classes directly in HTML structure instead of writing custom CSS.
component-extraction: If a combination of Tailwind classes is heavily repeated (like a button), extract it into a framework component (React/Vue/Svelte) rather than using `@apply` in CSS, to keep styling collocated.
**Tailwind v4:** Use `@apply` only for Tailwind utility names. Do not `@apply` custom/component class names â€” compilation can fail or behave unexpectedly.
configuration: Use `tailwind.config.js` to define your design system (colors, typography, spacing).
responsive-design: Think mobile-first. Write base utilities for mobile, and use prefixes (e.g., `md:`, `lg:`) for larger screens.

anti-patterns: âťŚ Overusing `@apply` instead of component abstractions.
âťŚ Writing arbitrary values (e.g., `w-[123px]`) instead of configuring the design system.

type-definitions: Prefer `interface` over `type` for object shapes as they are extensible.
null-checks: Rely on optional chaining (`?.`) and nullish coalescing (`??`).

anti-patterns: âťŚ Using `any`. Use `unknown` if the type is truly not known until runtime.
âťŚ Using `enum` for lean code. Prefer standard objects with `as const` instead.

features.stack: Vercel Functions, Edge Runtime, Vercel AI SDK, Vercel Postgres/Blob

rules: Use `export const config = { runtime: 'nodejs' }` explicitly if needed.
Always handle `Promise` resolution/rejection in proxy scenarios.

limits: Max duration: Must start responding in < 25s.
restrictions: âťŚ No Node.js native APIs (fs, child_process, net).
âťŚ No `eval()` or dynamic code execution.

security: Use `headers` section for CSP and security headers.

gateway: Use Vercel AI Gateway for unified access to multiple LLMs.
Enabled: Caching (cost savings) and Retry strategies.
sdk: Standardize on `ai` package (Vercel AI SDK) for stream-handling.

anti-patterns: âťŚ Using WebSockets in Serverless Functions (will disconnect).
âťŚ Long-running loops (>60s) in API routes.
âťŚ Hardcoding API keys (use Environment Variables).
âťŚ Local file system storage (temporary/ephemeral only).
âťŚ Forgetting `externalResolver: true` for third-party proxy libraries.

bundler: **Rolldown** (Rust) is the default bundler in Vite 8 â€” replaces esbuild + Rollup.
10-30x faster than Rollup, on par with esbuild.
Single unified devâ†’prod pipeline â€” no more two-bundler inconsistencies.
Rolldown 1.0 RC: API stable, passes 900+ Rollup and 670+ esbuild tests.
Built-in: TS/JSX transforms, ESM/CJS interop, CSS bundling (experimental).
environment-api: Vite Environment API defines multiple execution environments (client, server, edge).
Dev mirrors prod â€” develop against real server runtimes, not simulations.
Critical for Cloudflare Workers, edge runtimes, SSR.
Stabilizing in Vite 7; fully adopted in Vite 8.
nitro-integration: Nitro Vite plugin enables full-stack apps: auto SSR, bundled `index.html`, native Env API.
Vendor-agnostic: same code runs across environments without lock-in.
hmr: Leverage Vite's fast HMR â€” preserve component state during iterations.
Optimize cold starts: keep `vite.config.ts` lean, minimize plugin overhead.
config: Keep `vite.config.ts` minimal â€” rely on sensible defaults.

anti-patterns: âťŚ Using separate esbuild (dev) + Rollup (prod) in new projects (Rolldown unifies both).
âťŚ Ignoring dev/prod divergence â€” use Environment API to match runtimes.
âťŚ Heavy custom PostCSS chains degrading HMR speed.
âťŚ Over-configuring Vite â€” defaults are optimized.

stacks.name: VUE
security: Tree-shaking does NOT protect server code from leaking into client bundle.
Never import server-only modules in client components.
Never use `VITE_PUBLIC_` prefix for sensitive env vars.
All auth/authorization must happen on backend.

composition-api: Always use `<script setup>` and the Composition API for new components.
reactivity: Be deliberate between `ref` (for primitives and replacing object references) and `reactive` (for deep reactive objects).
Vue 3.6 uses **alien-signals** engine: 3-5x faster, 40% less memory, push-pull with lazy evaluation.
Fine-grained updates â€” only the changed part re-renders.
vapor-mode: Opt-in per component; compiles templates to direct DOM ops (no VDOM diffing).
Use for performance-critical components (lists, dashboards, animations).
Does NOT require changes to existing codebase â€” incremental adoption.
component-design: Keep components small, focused, and single-responsibility.
props: Use `defineProps` with TypeScript interfaces for strictly typed props.
state-management: **Pinia** is the canonical state management (replaces Vuex).
For local-first/offline: consider **rstore** (typed `useStore()`, offline sync, Drizzle/Directus).
routing: Vue Router treats URL as **typed state** (path, query, hash â†’ numbers, objects).
mcp-ai: `vue-mcp` Vite plugin exposes component tree, state, routes, Pinia to MCP servers.
For agentic AI copilots: use Vue composable + Pinia for guardrailed agent actions (Directus pattern).

anti-patterns: âťŚ Using Options API for new components.
âťŚ Mutating props directly.
âťŚ Using Vuex in new projects (use Pinia).
âťŚ Assuming tree-shaking protects sensitive server logic.
âťŚ Hardcoded API keys in client code.

concept: Vue's `@vue/reactivity` package works standalone â€” no browser needed.
Reactive source of truth on server â†’ auto fan-out to many clients.
patterns: **Reactive state â†’ SSE/WebSocket:** Server state changes trigger `effect()` â†’ push to clients via SSE or WebSocket.
**Computed projections:** `computed()` for per-user/per-role data views â€” only send what each client needs.
**Side effect management:** `watch()` and `effect()` handle scheduling, cleanup, and dependency tracking automatically.
**Event-driven:** Reactive effects replace manual event listeners for state-dependent logic.
setup: Import: `import { ref, computed, watch, effect } from '@vue/reactivity'`
Create reactive state, attach effects that push to connected clients.
use-cases: Real-time dashboards: server computes metrics â†’ push to all viewers.
Game state: single reactive truth â†’ fan-out to players via WebSocket.
IoT orchestration: sensor data triggers reactive effects â†’ commands to devices.
Multi-user collaboration: shared reactive state with per-user projections.

anti-patterns: âťŚ Using @vue/reactivity for heavy computation (it's for orchestration, not number crunching).
âťŚ Polling from clients when server can push via SSE/WebSocket.
âťŚ Manual event wiring when reactive effects auto-track dependencies.
âťŚ Forgetting cleanup â€” always dispose watchers/effects on disconnect.

integration-rules: Keep contract ABIs and addresses in a shared package or config.
Implement robust error handling for wallet rejections and network failures.
Ensure all sensitive on-chain interactions are confirmed by the user.
security-rules: Never store private keys in the frontend or environment variables exposed to the client.
Validate all incoming on-chain data before using it in the UI.
Implement transaction state tracking (pending, success, error) with user feedback.
anti-patterns: Trusting client-side state for transaction verification (always verify on-chain).
Hardcoding network IDs or contract addresses across the codebase.
